title: Epigastric pain radiating to the back

chart.oneLiner: 50M presents with 8 hours of severe constant epigastric pain radiating to the back, with nausea and vomiting, on a background of heavy alcohol use.

opening: I've got this terrible pain in the pit of my stomach that bores straight through to my back.
patientFile:
PERSONA: 50-year-old man, in significant pain, sitting forward/curled up. Cooperative but distracted by pain.

HPI: 8h of severe, constant epigastric pain boring through to the back, WORSE lying flat, somewhat better leaning forward. Persistent nausea and vomiting. Drank heavily over the weekend (only volunteers heavy alcohol if asked about alcohol). NO hematemesis, NO black stools, NO chest pain radiating to arm/jaw.
PMH: prior similar admission; hypertriglyceridemia (if asked). MEDS: none regularly. SOCIAL: drinks ~6–8 beers most days, escalating recently; smoker.

PHYSICAL EXAM (report ONLY what is requested; give normals when asked):
- General: distressed, diaphoretic, tachycardic.
- Vitals: BP 126/78, HR 108, RR 20, 37.9°C, SpO2 96% RA.
- Abdomen: marked epigastric tenderness with voluntary guarding; reduced bowel sounds; no rebound elsewhere; mild distension. No flank/periumbilical ecchymosis.
- Cardiac: tachycardic, regular. Lungs: clear (reduced left base if examined).

HIDDEN RULES:
- NEVER state or hint at a diagnosis. Answer only what is asked.
- Do NOT volunteer the heavy alcohol use or hypertriglyceridemia unless asked about alcohol, diet, or medical history.
- If asked something not in this file, give a reasonable normal/negative answer.

labs.Lipase: Lipase >3x the upper limit of normal — markedly elevated.
labs.LFTs: AST 70, ALT 60, bilirubin normal (alcohol pattern; ALT not >150 so gallstone cause less likely).
labs.CT_abdomen: CT (if obtained, usually not needed early): pancreatic edema/peripancreatic stranding; no necrosis on initial scan.

ekg.label: 12-lead EKG
ekg.findings: Tall peaked symmetric T waves, flattening/loss of P waves, broadening QRS.
ekg.verify: Source a study matching: Tall peaked symmetric T waves, flattening/loss of P waves, broadening QRS. Self-host with appropriate credit; do not reuse the prior normal tracing.
ekg.attribution: LITFL ECG Library (litfl.com) — © Life in the Fast Lane, CC BY-NC-SA 4.0. Educational/not-for-profit use, credit litfl.com; skip any image labelled third-party.

cxr.label: Chest X-ray (upright)
cxr.findings: Possible small left pleural effusion and basal atelectasis; no free air under the diaphragm.
cxr.verify: Pick a CC file showing a small basal/left pleural effusion or a normal upright film; must NOT show free air.
cxr.imageDescription: upright chest radiograph showing a small left pleural effusion with basal atelectasis
searchTerms: small left pleural effusion chest x-ray

steps.label: Differential
steps.prompt: Based on your history and physical, list your differential diagnosis (note your leading diagnosis).
steps.rubric: Acute pancreatitis (leading,8). Perforated peptic ulcer (CANT-MISS,4). Acute cholecystitis / choledocholithiasis (core,3). Inferior MI (CANT-MISS,3). Mesenteric ischemia (CANT-MISS,3). Ruptured AAA (bonus,2). Named leading dx w/ justification — epigastric-to-back pain, alcohol (core,2).
steps.idealAnswer: Leading diagnosis: Acute pancreatitis. Must-not-miss diagnoses to exclude: Perforated peptic ulcer; Inferior MI; Mesenteric ischemia. Also reasonable: Acute cholecystitis / choledocholithiasis.

coreActions.item: Named leading dx w/ justification — epigastric-to-back pain, alcohol

steps.label: Workup
steps.prompt: What initial diagnostic workup do you want to order?
steps.rubric: Lipase (CANT-MISS,5). CBC (2). BMP incl. calcium/glucose (3). LFTs (2). Triglycerides (1). Right upper quadrant ultrasound to assess gallstone etiology (1). EKG (1). PENALTY: routine early CT in clear-cut mild pancreatitis (low yield) (-1); shotgun panel w/ no reasoning (-2).

penalties.item: routine early CT in clear-cut mild pancreatitis (low yield)

contraindicatedTests: routine early CT in clear-cut mild pancreatitis (low yield)

steps.label: Read EKG
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's 12-lead EKG. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = a hyperkalemia pattern — tall peaked symmetric T waves, P-wave flattening/loss, and a broadening QRS. CREDIT: identifies the peaked T waves and QRS widening (7); recognizes this as a time-critical hyperkalemia pattern requiring urgent treatment (5); systematic read (3). PENALTY: calling the study normal / dismissing peaked T waves as a normal variant / missing the abnormality (-3).
keyFindings: recognizes a hyperkalemia pattern as urgent
criticalFindings: Hyperkalemia pattern: peaked T waves with QRS widening.
commonMistakes: dismissing peaked T waves as a normal variant
missing the loss of P waves
steps.idealAnswer: Hyperkalemia: tall peaked T waves, P-wave flattening, widening QRS — a time-critical pattern.

criticalActions: Hyperkalemia pattern: peaked T waves with QRS widening.
coreActions: recognizes a hyperkalemia pattern as urgent

penalties.label: calls the study normal / misses the abnormality

steps.label: Read CXR
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's chest X-ray. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = small basal/left effusion or atelectasis; NO free air. CREDIT: identifies a small effusion/atelectasis or a normal film (5); specifically excludes free air (perforation) (5). PENALTY: missing the importance of excluding free air (-1).
keyFindings: identifies a small effusion/atelectasis or a normal film
criticalFindings: Possible small left pleural effusion and basal atelectasis; no free air under the diaphragm.
commonMistakes: missing the importance of excluding free air
steps.idealAnswer: Possible small left pleural effusion and basal atelectasis; no free air under the diaphragm.

criticalActions.item: Recognizes: Possible small left pleural effusion and basal atelectasis; no free air under the diaphragm.

coreActions.item: identifies a small effusion/atelectasis or a normal film

penalties.item: missing the importance of excluding free air

steps.label: Revised Dx
steps.prompt: Given the labs, what is your diagnosis and how do you assess severity?
steps.rubric: Acute pancreatitis, likely alcohol-induced (5). Diagnosis = 2 of 3: characteristic pain, lipase >3x ULN, imaging (3). Assesses severity (BUN/creatinine, hemoconcentration, SIRS) (bonus,2).
steps.idealAnswer: Acute pancreatitis, likely alcohol-induced — diagnosis by the characteristic epigastric-to-back pain plus a lipase >3x the upper limit of normal (2 of 3 criteria). Assess severity with markers of volume depletion and end-organ stress: rising BUN/creatinine (BUN 26, Cr 1.3), hemoconcentration (Hgb 16), and SIRS.

steps.label: Management
steps.rubric: Per MGH pancreatitis (WATERFALL): controlled IV fluids – lactated Ringer's, titrate to BUN/HCT (CANT-MISS,7); analgesia (4); antiemetics (2); early enteral nutrition as tolerated (4); treat underlying cause / ERCP if gallstone + cholangitis (4); monitor for severity (SIRS, organ failure) (2). PENALTY: prophylactic antibiotics in uncomplicated pancreatitis (-3); prolonged NPO / over-aggressive late fluids (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Aggressive but controlled IV fluid resuscitation – lactated Ringer's preferred (avoid LR if hypercalcemia), ~3–4 L in first 24 h titrated to keep BUN/HCT from rising; avoid over-resuscitation after 24–48 h. Analgesia and antiemetics. Early enteral feeding as tolerated. Treat the cause (e.g. ERCP for gallstone pancreatitis with cholangitis). No prophylactic antibiotics.

coreActions.item: Treat underlying cause / ERCP if indicated

bonusActions.item: Lipid/triglyceride or calcium workup if indicated

optionalActions: Treat underlying cause / ERCP if indicated
Lipid/triglyceride or calcium workup if indicated

caseSummary.diagnosticFramework: 50M presents with 8 hours of severe constant epigastric pain radiating to the back, with nausea and vomiting, on a background of heavy alcohol use. The diagnosis is Acute pancreatitis, reached by eliciting the focused history, performing the targeted exam, and interpreting the directed workup while excluding the must-not-miss alternatives.

reasoningPathway: Frame the presentation from the chart and vitals.
Elicit the focused history, including sensitive items only on direct questioning.
Perform the targeted physical exam maneuvers to reveal key findings.
Build a differential led by Acute pancreatitis while explicitly excluding must-not-miss causes.
Order a focused, order-sensitive workup and interpret EKG/CXR and labs.
Commit to Acute pancreatitis and manage with stabilization, definitive treatment, consults, and disposition.
